rulings.Case Title: RONALD D. CARTER VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Set A Hearing For Writ Of Mandamus Application
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, February 21, 2025, line 1, PETITIONER RONALD CARTER'S Motion To Set A Hearing For Writ Of Mandamus Application Petitioner Ronald Carter filed a petition for writ of mandamus on May 1, 2024. It does not appear he served a summons in this action on the named respondent, the State of California. With the present motion, he seeks a hearing on his writ. This motion appears to have been served by mail on the Secretary of State. "Service of summons in all actions on claims against the state shall be made on the Attorney General." (Gov. Code, 955.4.) Because there is no proof of service of the petition or this motion on the Attorney General, the motion is denied. (See Cal. Rule of Court 3.1300(a).) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address = (301/CVA)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Bernie Schachter, And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, February 21, 2025, line 7, PLAINTIFF JOHN BROSNAN'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Bernie Schachter The motion to compel Bernie Schachter's compliance with Plaintiff's deposition subpoena is denied. In Plaintiff's moving papers, filed on January 23, 2025, he provides a proof of service of deposition subpoena on Schachter that is dated August 21, 2024. (Opening Br. at 5.) The date specified for deposition was September 20, 2024. (Abraham Dec. para 33.) Schachter objected on August 22 and did not appear. The present motion was filed more than 60 days after the date of objections and the date of the deposition and is therefore untimely. (CCP 2025.480(b).) Plaintiff's reply brief states that he has now served a new deposition subpoena on Schachter, apparently seeking the same items. That subpoena is not properly before the court, but even assuming Plaintiff could cure his delay in moving to compel, the court finds that Schachter's objections have merit. Plaintiff has not filed a separate statement in support of his motion. (See Rule of Court 3.1345.) He has not stated any good cause why the court should compel production; his reply brief states why he should have prevailed in the already-litigated action Brosnan v. JSB Equities LP et al., but does not discuss the relevance of Schachter's testimony or the items he has sought through subpoena to his new action against his former attorney. CCP 2025.480(j) provides that the court "shall" impose a monetary sanction on any party who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a discovery motion like the one at issue unless it finds that the party acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of sanction unjust. The court finds that Brosnan acted without substantial justification here but reduces the amount awarded in light of all the circumstances here. The court awards $3,000 in sanctions to Schachter, payable by Plaintiff via Schachter's attorney, within 30 days of notice of entry of this order. Schachter also seeks a stay of discovery against him. The court denies this request without prejudice to any properly noticed motion for protective order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)
